stimulus.textEn: The Earth's rigid outer shell is not a single unbroken layer but a set of enormous plates that fit together like pieces of a cracked shell. These plates rest on hotter, slowly flowing rock beneath them and drift a few centimeters each year, about as fast as a fingernail grows. Most dramatic geological events happen at their boundaries. Where two plates pull apart, usually along ridges on the ocean floor, molten rock rises from below, cools, and hardens into new crust, gradually widening the ocean between them. Where plates collide, one may bend and plunge beneath the other into the depths, or the two may crumple upward, raising mountain ranges over millions of years. Where plates grind sideways past each other, the built-up strain is released in sudden jolts we feel as earthquakes. The idea that continents move was once dismissed, since no one could explain what force could shift them. The discovery that the seafloor itself spreads outward from mid-ocean ridges finally provided the missing mechanism and transformed our understanding of how the planet's surface is continually remade.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happens where two plates pull apart?

choices.text: One plate plunges beneath the other.
Molten rock rises and hardens into new crust.
The plates grind sideways past each other.

items.prompt: What does the passage say causes earthquakes at some boundaries?

choices.text: Molten rock cooling on the ocean floor
New crust forming at the ridges
Strain released as plates grind sideways past each other
The slow upward growth of mountain ranges

items.prompt: How fast do the plates drift, according to the passage?

choices.text: A few centimeters each year, about as fast as a fingernail grows

stimulus.textEn: The oceans are in constant motion, and some of their currents form a vast, connected system that moves heat around the globe. Near the surface, winds drive great streams of warm water from the tropics toward the poles, warming the air above coastal lands along the way. As this water travels into cold regions, two things happen: it loses heat to the atmosphere, and, where sea ice forms, it grows saltier, because ice leaves its salt behind. Cold, salty water is dense, and dense water sinks. In a few key locations, surface water plunges to the deep ocean and begins a slow return journey along the bottom, eventually rising again elsewhere to complete the loop. This overturning circulation, driven by differences in temperature and saltiness, may take a thousand years to finish a single cycle. Its influence is enormous, for it helps regulate regional climates and distributes nutrients through the sea. Scientists watch it closely because they suspect that a large influx of fresh water from melting ice could weaken this circulation, with consequences felt far beyond the ocean itself.

items.prompt: According to the passage, why does surface water sink in certain cold regions?

choices.text: Because winds physically push it downward
Because it becomes warmer and lighter
Because it becomes cold and salty, and therefore dense
Because dissolved nutrients weigh it down

items.prompt: How does the formation of sea ice affect the remaining water?

choices.text: It makes the water saltier by leaving salt behind.
It removes all the salt from the water.
It speeds the water back toward the tropics.

items.prompt: About how long may a single cycle of this circulation take?

stimulus.textEn: Cities are often several degrees warmer than the countryside around them, a phenomenon known as the urban heat island. The difference arises largely from the materials that cover urban surfaces. Asphalt, concrete, and brick absorb sunlight during the day and store its heat, then release it slowly through the evening, keeping streets warm long after sunset. Tall buildings add to the effect by trapping heat within narrow spaces and blocking breezes that might carry it away. Meanwhile, cities contain far less vegetation than rural areas. Trees and grass cool their surroundings by shading the ground and releasing water vapor, and where they are replaced by pavement, that natural cooling is lost. Vehicles, factories, and air conditioners add still more heat directly. The consequences reach beyond mere discomfort: higher temperatures increase energy demand for cooling and can worsen air quality and heat-related illness. Planners have found that relatively simple measures, such as planting trees, creating parks, and using lighter-colored roofing that reflects sunlight, can meaningfully reduce the warming and make dense neighborhoods more livable.

items.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?

choices.text: Why cities are warmer than surrounding areas and how the effect can be reduced
Why the countryside is warmer than nearby cities
How air conditioners cool an entire city
Why planting trees makes cities hotter

items.prompt: According to the passage, how do trees and grass cool their surroundings?

choices.text: By absorbing and storing heat like concrete
By shading the ground and releasing water vapor
By trapping heat within narrow spaces

items.prompt: Which measure does the passage mention for reducing urban heat?

choices.text: Using lighter-colored roofing that reflects sunlight

stimulus.textEn: Sleep is not a uniform state of blank unconsciousness but a structured cycle that the brain repeats several times each night. Researchers, tracking the electrical activity of the brain, have identified two broad kinds of sleep. In non-REM sleep, brain waves slow and deepen, the body relaxes, and physical repair is thought to take place. This is followed by REM sleep, named for the rapid eye movements that occur beneath closed lids. During REM, the brain becomes almost as active as when awake, and most vivid dreaming happens. A full cycle lasts roughly ninety minutes, and the proportions shift as the night wears on, with deep non-REM dominating early and longer REM periods toward morning. Far from being wasted time, sleep supports functions the waking brain cannot easily perform. Waste products that accumulate between brain cells during the day are cleared away more efficiently during sleep, and disrupting the cycle repeatedly leaves people not only tired but impaired in mood, judgment, and concentration.

items.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Sleep is a structured cycle that serves important functions
Dreams reveal a person's hidden wishes and fears
People need exactly ninety minutes of sleep each night
REM sleep is the only stage of sleep that matters

items.prompt: REM sleep takes its name from

choices.text: the deep, slow brain waves that occur during it
the rapid eye movements that occur during it
the physical repair that happens in it
the removal of waste from the brain

items.prompt: According to the passage, as the night continues,

choices.text: sleep becomes lighter until the person suddenly wakes
the ninety-minute cycle grows steadily longer
REM periods become longer toward morning

stimulus.textEn: Beneath most forests lies a hidden partnership between trees and fungi that has shaped plant life for millions of years. Fine fungal threads, called hyphae, wrap around or penetrate plant roots, forming an association known as mycorrhiza. The fungus extends far into the soil, reaching water and minerals such as phosphorus that the roots alone could not obtain. In return, the plant supplies the fungus with sugars produced during photosynthesis. This exchange benefits both partners so reliably that most land plants form such relationships. Recent studies suggest the fungal threads may link neighboring trees into a shared network. Through these connections, carbon and warning signals can pass from one plant to another, allowing older trees to support struggling seedlings nearby. Some scientists caution that the extent of this cooperation is still debated, and that fungi may act partly in their own interest. Even so, the discovery has transformed how researchers picture a forest, revealing an underground economy of trade rather than a collection of isolated individuals.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does the fungus provide to the plant?

choices.text: Water and minerals such as phosphorus

items.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Fungi harm trees by stealing sugars from their roots.
Photosynthesis takes place mainly in the roots of trees.
Trees and fungi form a mutually beneficial partnership that may connect a forest.
A forest is simply a collection of completely isolated individual trees.

items.prompt: The passage states that through fungal connections, trees may

choices.text: produce many more flowers each spring.
grow taller than all surrounding plants.
pass carbon and warning signals to one another.

stimulus.textEn: Sand dunes are sculpted wherever loose sand, wind, and open space come together. The wind lifts and rolls individual grains across the surface, and when moving air meets an obstacle, such as a shrub or a slight rise, it slows and drops part of its load. Sand begins to pile up, and the growing mound itself becomes an obstacle that traps still more. A dune typically develops two distinct faces. On the windward side, the gentler slope, the wind pushes sand upward toward the crest. On the far, steeper side, sheltered from the wind, the grains tumble down and settle. Because sand is continually removed from one side and deposited on the other, the whole dune slowly migrates downwind, sometimes advancing several meters in a year and burying whatever lies in its path. The shape a dune takes depends on the supply of sand, the strength of the wind, and the vegetation present. Where plants take root, their tangled roots can anchor the sand and halt the dune's march.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what causes the wind to drop the sand it is carrying?

choices.text: The sand suddenly becomes too heavy to lift.
The wind changes direction every night.
Rain washes the grains out of the air.
The wind slows when it meets an obstacle.

items.prompt: What happens on the steeper, sheltered side of a dune?

choices.text: The grains tumble down and settle.
The wind pushes sand up toward the crest.
Plants always take root there first.
The sand hardens into solid rock.

items.prompt: It can be inferred that a dune migrates downwind because

choices.text: the wind blows it whole across the ground.
sand is removed from one side and deposited on the other.
its roots pull it in the wind's direction.

stimulus.textEn: Although water covers most of the Earth's surface, only a tiny fraction is fresh and available for human use. Much of that supply lies hidden underground in aquifers, layers of porous rock and sediment that hold water in their spaces. People reach it by drilling wells, and in many dry regions such groundwater is the main source of drinking water and irrigation. Aquifers are refilled slowly as rain and melting snow seep down through the soil, a process that may take years, centuries, or longer. Trouble arises when water is withdrawn faster than it is replaced. Water tables fall, wells must be drilled deeper, and the ground itself can sink as the emptied spaces collapse. Along coasts, over-pumping may draw salty seawater into the aquifer, spoiling the freshwater it once held. Because some large aquifers were filled during wetter climates thousands of years ago, the water they contain is, in practical terms, nonrenewable. Managing these hidden reserves wisely has become essential in regions where they support agriculture and cities that could not otherwise exist.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how are aquifers refilled?

choices.text: By pumping water back down through wells
By seawater flowing in from the coast
By rivers draining directly into the wells
By rain and melting snow seeping through the soil

items.prompt: What can happen when water is withdrawn faster than it is replaced?

choices.text: The ground can sink and water tables fall.
The aquifer refills much more quickly.
The water becomes purer over time.
Rainfall in the region increases sharply.

stimulus.textEn: Listen to part of a lecture in an art history class. When we admire a wall painting from the Renaissance, it's worth asking how it was actually made. There were two main techniques. The first, buon fresco, means 'true fresco.' The artist spread a layer of wet plaster on the wall and painted directly into it with pigments mixed in water. As the plaster dried, a chemical reaction sealed the color into the wall itself. The result is remarkably durable—the paint becomes part of the surface. But there's a catch: the artist had to work fast, finishing a section before the plaster dried, with no chance to correct mistakes. The second method, fresco secco, means 'dry fresco.' Here the artist painted onto plaster that had already dried, using pigment mixed with a binder like egg. This was more forgiving and allowed finer detail, but the paint sat on the surface rather than bonding with it. Over centuries, secco painting tends to flake and fade. That's why the best-preserved wall paintings are usually true frescoes.

items.prompt: In buon fresco, what does the artist paint onto?

choices.text: Dry plaster with an egg binder

items.prompt: According to the professor, what is a disadvantage of buon fresco?

choices.text: The colors fade within a few years
The artist must work quickly with no corrections
It can only be used for small paintings

items.prompt: Why are true frescoes usually better preserved than secco paintings?

choices.text: They were painted by more skilled artists
They were kept indoors more often
The pigment bonds into the wall itself

stimulus.textEn: Listen to part of a biology lecture. Professor: In many forests, fungi connect with plant roots in a relationship called mycorrhiza. The fungus helps the plant absorb minerals from the soil, especially phosphorus. In return, the plant supplies the fungus with sugars produced during photosynthesis. This exchange can benefit both organisms, but it is not always perfectly balanced. If soil nutrients are abundant, a plant may depend less on the fungus. If light is limited, the plant may produce fewer sugars, which affects the fungus. So the relationship is mutualistic, but the strength of the benefit changes with environmental conditions. Ecologists study these shifts across different forest types over time.

A type of soil that contains no minerals

choices.text: Environmental conditions can change how much each organism benefits.
Fungi never help plants absorb minerals.
Plants stop producing sugars in every forest.
The relationship occurs only in laboratory experiments.

stimulus.textEn: Listen to part of a lecture in a chemistry class. Living cells run thousands of chemical reactions, and most of them, left alone, would happen far too slowly to keep you alive. The solution is a catalyst—a substance that speeds up a reaction without being used up itself. In cells, these catalysts are proteins called enzymes. Here's the basic idea. Every reaction has to climb over an energy barrier before it can proceed, like pushing a boulder over a hill. An enzyme works by lowering that barrier. It doesn't change how much energy the reaction releases overall; it just provides an easier path. It does this by grabbing hold of the reacting molecules and holding them in exactly the right position to react. Because the enzyme itself emerges unchanged, a single enzyme molecule can perform the same trick over and over, thousands of times a second. One more crucial point: enzymes are highly specific. Each one is shaped to fit only certain molecules, like a lock and key. That specificity lets the cell control which reactions speed up and which don't—a kind of chemical management system.

items.prompt: What is a catalyst?

choices.text: A molecule destroyed during the reaction
A substance that speeds a reaction without being used up

items.prompt: How does an enzyme speed up a reaction?

choices.text: By raising the temperature of the cell
By becoming part of the product

items.prompt: Why does the professor mention a lock and key?

choices.text: To show enzymes are easily broken
To explain that enzymes act only on specific molecules
To prove enzymes are used up quickly
To argue that all enzymes are identical

stimulus.textEn: Listen to part of a lecture in a psychology class. Let's talk about how the timing of study affects memory. Suppose you have four hours to prepare for an exam. You could study all four hours in one sitting—that's called massed practice, or cramming. Or you could break those four hours into shorter sessions spread across several days. Decades of research point to a clear winner: spreading it out. This is known as the spacing effect. When you space your study, you tend to remember more, and for longer, than when you cram the same total amount. Why? One idea is that each time you return to the material after a gap, your brain has partly forgotten it, so retrieving it again requires effort—and that effort strengthens the memory. Cramming, by contrast, feels productive because the information stays fresh in the moment, but that fluency is misleading. It fades quickly. So the uncomfortable truth is that the study method that feels easiest often teaches you the least.

items.prompt: What is 'massed practice'?

choices.text: Studying in short sessions over many days
Studying all at once in a single sitting

items.prompt: According to the professor, what strengthens memory during spaced study?

choices.text: The effort of retrieving partly forgotten material

items.prompt: What does the professor mean by saying the easiest method 'often teaches you the least'?

choices.text: Cramming feels effective but produces weaker memory
Students should avoid studying before exams
Spacing requires more total hours than cramming

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on technology in education. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Castellano: Some schools are introducing AI tutoring systems that adapt lessons to each student. Do you think such personalized software improves learning more than a human teacher leading the whole class, or does it lose something important?

Samir: Personalized AI tutoring is more effective because it lets every student move at their own pace instead of waiting for the rest of the class.

Tara: I think a human teacher matters more; they notice when students are confused or discouraged in ways software can't, and that emotional support drives real learning.
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：自适应的 AI 辅导软件是否比人类教师带全班授课更能促进学习，还是会失去重要的东西？回应教授并就 Samir/Tara 的不同观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：清楚表明更看重 AI 个性化辅导、人类教师，还是两者结合。
理由/例证支撑：用具体理由或例子说明，如学习节奏、情感支持、动机激励、规模化等。
回应至少一位同学：明确呼应 Samir 或 Tara 的观点，表示同意、反驳或补充。
组织连贯：先亮明观点再展开论证，使用衔接词使段落逻辑清晰。
语法与学术词汇：句式多样，准确使用 personalized、adaptive、engagement 等学术词汇，语法错误少。

items.prompt: You are enrolled in a large Psychology lecture that also has weekly small discussion sections. You were placed in the Friday afternoon discussion section, but it now overlaps with a part-time job you have started. Write an email to the course coordinator. In your email, explain why your current discussion section no longer works for you, request to be moved to a Tuesday or Thursday section instead, and ask what steps you need to take to make the change.
items.promptZh: 给课程协调员写邮件：你被分到心理学课的周五下午讨论小组，但现在与你新开始的兼职工作时间冲突。说明当前讨论组时间不再合适、请求换到周二或周四的小组、询问办理调换需要哪些步骤。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 称呼/语域：对协调员使用得体的正式称呼与礼貌请求语气，体现配合意愿。
任务完成度：须涵盖三项动作——说明现讨论组时间冲突原因、请求调到周二或周四、询问办理调换的步骤。
组织连贯：从说明缘由到提出具体调换请求再到询问流程，结构清晰连贯。
语法准确：正确使用现在时与现在完成时描述现状，被动/情态结构（could I be moved）表达委婉请求。
词汇得体：合理使用 discussion section、overlap/conflict、coordinator、enroll 等校园词汇。

items.prompt: A research lab you are interested in is holding an open house for prospective student members. Write an email to the lab coordinator. In your email, say you would like to attend the open house, ask whether you need to sign up in advance, and ask what kind of projects students in the lab work on.
items.promptZh: 你感兴趣的研究实验室将办开放日面向有意加入的学生，写信给实验室协调员表示想参加、询问是否需提前报名、并问实验室学生做哪类项目。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 语域：写给实验室协调员，语气礼貌、积极、专业。
任务完成度：须完成三个动作——表示想参加开放日、询问是否需提前报名、询问实验室学生所做的项目类型。
组织连贯：意愿—报名方式—项目了解顺序自然。
语法准确：情态动词与疑问句（whether/what kind of）使用准确。
词汇得体：恰当使用 open house、attend、sign up、projects 等词。

items.prompt: You are a student who relies on the free campus shuttle bus to travel between the main campus and the science campus. Recently the shuttle has been arriving late and sometimes leaving early, so you have missed it several times. Write an email to the transportation services office. In your email, report the problem with examples, explain how it affects your daily schedule, and politely ask whether the timetable could be reviewed or made more reliable.
items.promptZh: 给交通服务办公室写邮件：你依赖免费校园班车往返主校区与理科校区，但班车近期常晚到、有时早走，让你多次错过。举例说明问题、解释对你日程的影响、并礼貌询问能否检查或改善时刻表的可靠性。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 称呼/语域：收件人是交通服务办公室，应使用正式称呼，语气礼貌克制，即便反映问题也不带攻击性。
任务完成度：完成三个动作——举例报告班车晚到/早走问题、说明对日常日程的影响、礼貌询问能否复核或改善时刻表。
组织连贯：先陈述问题并配以具体例子，再说明影响，最后提出询问，信息层次分明、衔接自然。
语法准确：描述近期反复发生的问题恰当使用现在完成时/一般现在时，被动语态（could be reviewed）使用正确，句子完整。
词汇得体：准确使用 shuttle/timetable/schedule/reliable/on time/affect 等交通与时间相关词汇，用词精准不啰嗦。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on consumer minimalism. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Quinn: Some argue that owning less leads to a happier, more sustainable life, while others see consumption as a driver of progress and joy. Is a minimalist lifestyle genuinely better, or is it an unrealistic ideal? Share your view.

Tara: It really is better. Buying less reduces waste and stress, and people often find they value experiences over accumulating things.

Kenji: I'm skeptical. Minimalism is a privilege of those who already have enough, and consumer spending sustains jobs and innovation.
items.promptZh: 教授问极简生活是否真的更好；Tara 认为少买减少浪费与压力、更重体验，Kenji 认为极简是有余者的特权，而消费支撑就业与创新。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：明确支持或质疑极简生活方式
理由与例证：以可持续性或经济作用等理由支撑
回应同学：回应 Tara 或 Kenji 的论点
组织连贯：论证层次分明、衔接自然
语法与学术词汇：语法准确并使用学术词汇

items.prompt: You would like Professor Lee to supervise your thesis and want to meet to discuss it. Write an email to Professor Lee. In your email, introduce yourself and your research interests, explain why you would like Professor Lee to advise your thesis, and ask to arrange a first meeting.
items.promptZh: 你希望Lee教授指导你的论文，写信自我介绍与研究兴趣、说明为何希望其指导、并请求安排第一次会面。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 语域：写给教授，语气正式、尊敬且得体。
任务完成度：须完成三个动作——自我介绍与研究兴趣、说明希望其担任论文导师的原因、请求安排首次会面。
组织连贯：自我介绍—说明原因—请求会面，逻辑连贯。
语法准确：情态动词（would like to）与从句结构使用准确。
词汇得体：准确使用 thesis、supervise/advise、research interests、arrange a meeting 等词。

items.prompt: You are a student who tried to enroll in a popular seminar that was already full, so the system placed you on the waitlist several weeks ago. The term begins soon and you have not heard anything about your position on the list, which makes it hard to finalize the rest of your schedule. Write an email to the Registration Office. In your email, explain that you have been on the waitlist for this seminar and have not received an update, ask where you currently stand on the list and how likely you are to get a place, and ask by what date you should make a backup plan if a seat does not open up.
items.promptZh: 给注册办公室写邮件：你几周前因热门研讨课已满被排入候补名单，临近开学仍未收到任何关于排位的消息，影响其余课表安排，说明你已在候补名单且无进展、询问当前排位与录取可能性、询问若无名额应在何时前准备备选方案。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 称呼/语域：正式礼貌地询问候补进展，语气耐心、不催促失礼，开头结尾规范。
任务完成度：须说明已在候补名单且久无更新、询问当前排位与录取可能、询问准备备选方案的时间节点，三项动作齐全。
组织连贯：按"现状—进展询问—时间节点"顺序衔接，使用 since/because/if 等连接词使逻辑清晰。
语法准确：恰当使用现在完成时(have been on the waitlist/have not received)与条件句(if a seat does not open up)，时态准确。
词汇得体：准确使用 waitlist/enroll/seminar/position on the list/backup plan 等注册场景词汇，措辞得体。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on grading systems. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Professor Bennett: In curved grading, your final grade depends partly on how your classmates perform rather than on a fixed standard. Is grading on a curve a fair way to evaluate students, or does it create unhealthy competition? Share your view.

Priya: A curve is fair. It corrects for exams that are too hard or too easy and keeps standards consistent across sections.

Daniel: I see it differently. A curve pits classmates against one another and punishes strong students who happen to be in a talented group.
items.promptZh: 教授问按曲线评分是否公平；Priya 认为曲线能校正难度、保持标准一致，Daniel 认为它制造恶性竞争并惩罚身处强班的学生。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：明确支持或反对曲线评分
理由与例证：以公平性或竞争压力等具体理由支撑
回应同学：回应 Priya 或 Daniel 的观点
组织连贯：论证层次分明、衔接自然
语法与学术词汇：语法准确并使用学术性词汇

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on environmental policy. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Adeyemi: Countries often face a choice between encouraging economic growth and protecting the environment. When the two conflict, which should be the higher priority?

Ravi: Economic growth should come first. It creates jobs and lifts people out of poverty, which improves lives right now.

Elena: I'd put the environment first. Without clean air, water, and a stable climate, long-term prosperity is impossible.
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：经济增长与环境保护冲突时何者优先？回应教授并就 Ravi/Elena 的观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 明确表明优先增长、优先环境或寻求平衡
给出具体理由或例子支撑
回应 Ravi 或 Elena 的论点
使用多样的学术词汇与连接词
写成连贯段落，至少约 100 词

items.prompt: You are thinking about dropping one of your courses this semester because the workload has become too heavy. Write an email to your academic advisor. In your email, explain your situation, ask whether dropping the course would affect your graduation, and request a short meeting to discuss your options.
items.promptZh: 给学业导师写邮件：因课业太重想退掉一门课，说明情况、询问是否影响毕业、并请求面谈讨论选择。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 以正式语气称呼导师
说明想退课的原因与课程名称
询问退课是否影响按时毕业
请求一次简短面谈
礼貌结尾并署名

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on how research is funded. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Okeke: Funding agencies must decide where to put limited research money. When resources are tight, should agencies give priority to basic research driven by curiosity, or to applied research aimed at solving a known practical problem?

Ivar: I think basic research should come first. Many of today's technologies grew out of curiosity-driven discoveries that had no obvious use at the time they were made.

Juno: I disagree. When money is limited, agencies should fund applied projects that address pressing problems now, since people benefit much sooner from research with a cle
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：经费有限时，资助机构应优先支持以好奇心驱动的基础研究，还是面向已知实际问题的应用研究？回应教授并就 Ivar/Juno 的不同观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：清楚说明你更支持优先基础研究、优先应用研究，还是按情况平衡两者的立场。
理由/例证支撑：从长期回报、即时收益、不确定性或社会需求等角度给出具体理由与例子。
回应至少一位同学：明确呼应 Ivar 或 Juno 的观点，表示赞同、反驳或加以补充延伸。
组织连贯：先表态再展开理由，使用恰当的连接词处理对比与让步，段落层次清晰。
语法与学术词汇：句式多样、用词准确，恰当使用如 funding、application、breakthrough 等学术词汇并控制语法错误。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on fast fashion. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Professor Keller: Fast fashion offers cheap, trendy clothes but generates enormous waste and pollution. Should consumers and governments actively discourage fast fashion, or is affordable clothing a benefit worth its costs? Explain.

Amara: We should discourage it. The environmental and labor costs are severe, and a culture of disposable clothing is unsustainable.

Dylan: I'd defend it. Cheap clothing makes style accessible to people on low incomes, and the answer is better production, not guilt.
items.promptZh: 教授问是否应抑制快时尚；Amara 认为其环境与劳工代价严重、一次性穿衣文化不可持续，Dylan 认为廉价服装让低收入者也能追求时尚，出路在于改进生产而非道德指责。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：明确支持或反对抑制快时尚
理由与例证：以环境劳工代价或可及性等理由支撑
回应同学：回应 Amara 或 Dylan 的论点
组织连贯：结构完整、逻辑清晰
语法与学术词汇：语法准确并使用学术表达

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on exam formats. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Professor Novak: In open-book exams, students may consult their notes and texts during the test. Do open-book exams measure understanding better than closed-book ones, or do they let students avoid truly learning the material? Defend your stance.

Ana: Open-book exams are better. Real life lets you look things up, so testing how you apply information matters more than memorization.

Ruben: I think they're worse. Without pressure to internalize material, many students skim and never build the foundation they need later.
items.promptZh: 教授问开卷考试是否更能衡量理解；Ana 认为应用能力比记忆更重要，Ruben 认为缺少内化压力会让学生只浮于表面、基础薄弱。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：明确支持开卷或闭卷考试
理由与例证：以应用能力或知识内化等理由支撑
回应同学：回应 Ana 或 Ruben 的观点
组织连贯：结构清晰、逻辑连贯
语法与学术词汇：语法准确并使用学术表达

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on advertising and influencer marketing. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Underwood: Many social-media creators are paid to feature products without always making the arrangement obvious to their followers. Should influencers be required to clearly label every paid promotion, even when doing so might make the post feel less natural?

Benji: I think clear labels should be required. Followers trust creators as if they were friends, so hiding that money changed hands is a kind of deception that unfairly influences buying decisions.

Cira: I see it differently. Audiences today already assume that popular creators earn money from brands
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：网红是否应清晰标注每条付费推广内容，即使这会让帖子显得不够自然？回应教授并就 Benji/Cira 的不同观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：清楚说明你更支持强制标注付费推广、反对繁琐标注，还是设条件的折中立场。
理由/例证支撑：从信任、欺骗风险、消费者判断力或帖子观感等角度给出具体理由与例子。
回应至少一位同学：明确呼应 Benji 或 Cira 的观点，表示赞同、反驳或加以补充延伸。
组织连贯：先表态再展开理由，使用恰当连接词处理对比与让步，段落层次清晰。
语法与学术词汇：恰当使用如 disclosure、transparency、credibility 等学术词汇，句式多样且语法基本准确。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on who collects scientific data. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Salgado: Some projects now invite ordinary volunteers to help gather observations, such as counting birds or recording local weather. Do you think large research projects benefit from involving untrained volunteers, or should data collection be left to trained specialists?

Orla: Involving volunteers is a great idea. Thousands of people can gather observations across a huge area that a small team of specialists could never cover on their own.

Priscilla: I disagree. Untrained volunteers may record things inconsistently, and cleaning up unreliable data can cost more e
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：大型研究项目应让未受训练的志愿者参与数据采集，还是应交由受过训练的专业人员完成？回应教授并就 Orla/Priscilla 的不同观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：清楚表明你更支持引入志愿者、专业人员主导，还是两者结合的立场。
理由/例证支撑：从覆盖范围、规模、数据一致性或可靠性等角度给出具体理由与例子。
回应至少一位同学：明确呼应 Orla 或 Priscilla 的观点，表示赞同、反驳或补充。
组织连贯：观点与理由层层展开，过渡自然，段落结构完整。
语法与学术词汇：恰当使用如 volunteer、observation、accuracy 等词，句式多样且语法基本准确。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on tipping practices. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Imani: In some countries, tipping is expected, while in others it is built into wages. Should tipping be replaced by fair fixed wages, or does tipping reward good service? Share your view.

Cleo: I'd replace it. Tips make income unstable and pressure customers, so fair wages built into the price are more honest.

Rory: I'd keep it. Tipping directly rewards good service and gives motivated workers a chance to earn more than a flat wage.
items.promptZh: 教授问小费文化是否应被固定工资取代；Cleo 认为小费使收入不稳、给顾客压力，纳入价格的工资更诚实，Rory 认为小费直接奖励优质服务、让积极的员工挣得更多。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：明确支持取消小费或保留小费
理由与例证：以收入稳定或激励服务等理由支撑
回应同学：回应 Cleo 或 Rory 的论点
组织连贯：论证连贯、层次清晰
语法与学术词汇：语法准确并使用学术词汇

items.prompt: Your student club is holding an evening event and needs to borrow a projector for it. Write an email to the campus media services office. In your email, request to borrow a projector for a specific date, ask how far in advance you must book it, and ask whether you need to return it the same night.
items.promptZh: 你的社团办晚间活动需借投影仪，写信给校园媒体服务处申请指定日期借用、询问需提前多久预订、并问是否须当晚归还。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 语域：写给校园媒体服务处，语气礼貌、清楚。
任务完成度：须完成三个动作——申请在指定日期借用投影仪、询问需提前多久预订、询问是否须当晚归还。
组织连贯：借用请求—预订时限—归还要求顺序清楚。
语法准确：情态动词请求与疑问句（how far in advance/whether）使用准确。
词汇得体：恰当使用 projector、borrow、book in advance、return 等词。

items.prompt:
Your professor is teaching a class on data privacy and consumer technology. Write a post responding to the professor's question. In your response, express and support your own opinion, and add to the discussion in your own words.

Dr. Abadi: Many free apps and websites fund themselves by collecting and selling users' personal data. Should governments ban this business model, or is allowing users to trade their data for free services a fair exchange?

Quentin: I think the trade is fair as long as it's disclosed; people who don't want ads or tracking can pay for premium versions or use other tools.

Rosa: I disagree. Most users can't really understand what they're giving up, so the exchange isn't truly informed, and the practice should be restricted.
items.promptZh: 学术讨论：政府是否应禁止靠收集并出售用户数据来提供免费服务的商业模式，还是这属于公平交换？回应教授并就 Quentin/Rosa 的不同观点表态。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 立场明确：清楚表明应禁止、允许，还是有条件允许（如强制透明）。
理由/例证支撑：用具体理由或例子说明，如知情同意、用户选择权、数据滥用风险等。
回应至少一位同学：明确呼应 Quentin 或 Rosa 的观点，表示同意、反驳或补充。
组织连贯：先亮明观点再展开论证，使用衔接词使段落逻辑清晰。
语法与学术词汇：句式多样，准确使用 privacy、consent、disclosure 等学术词汇，语法错误少。

items.prompt: You are applying to a graduate program and want feedback on your personal statement from the writing center. Write an email to the writing center. In your email, request a consultation about your personal statement, mention the application deadline you are working toward, and ask whether you should send your draft before the session.
items.promptZh: 你申请研究生项目想请写作中心看个人陈述，写信预约咨询、说明申请截止日期、并询问是否需提前发送草稿。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 语域：写给写作中心，语气礼貌、专业。
任务完成度：须完成三个动作——预约个人陈述咨询、说明申请截止日期、询问是否需在咨询前发送草稿。
组织连贯：预约—时间背景—材料准备顺序自然。
语法准确：情态动词请求与现在进行时（am applying）使用准确。
词汇得体：准确使用 personal statement、consultation、deadline、draft 等词。

items.prompt: Your university's photography club is holding an exhibition next month and has asked for volunteers. Write an email to the club president. In your email, offer to help, explain what skills or experience you can contribute, and ask what tasks still need to be done.
items.promptZh: 给摄影社社长写邮件：下月办展览招募志愿者，表示愿意帮忙、说明自己能贡献的技能或经验、并询问还有哪些工作需要做。

answer.note: Reference points only; score is an estimate, never official.
referencePoints: 友好礼貌地称呼社长
明确表示愿意做志愿者
说明自己能贡献的相关技能或经验
询问目前还需要哪些帮助
礼貌结尾并署名

items.prompt: 彻底的，详尽的
diffident
合群的，喜爱社交的
掩饰；与……不符（动词）
谨慎的，慎重的
superfluous
谨慎的，慎重的
candid
notorious
从前，以前（副词）
深刻的，深远的
pervasive
（门、抽屉的）球形把手；旋钮（名词）
quench
exemplary
novice
时间的；世俗的（形容词）
broaden
钻研，细看；毛孔（动词/名词）
后退，逐渐远去
职业，天职
坚定的，忠实的
jeopardize
雄辩的，有口才的
完全的，彻底的（形容词）
逗留，徘徊；缓慢消失（动词）
ebullient
反复无常的，任性的
史无前例的，前所未有的
逐渐减少，缩小（动词）

stimulus.textEn: The river that once powered dozens of mills now runs quietly past their empty brick shells. But the same current that turned the great wooden wheels has been harnessed once more, this time spinning turbines that light the very town the mills first built. The water works on; only its purpose has changed.

items.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: The town has been abandoned ever since the mills closed.
The river still serves the town, though its use has shifted from mills to electricity.
The river is no longer of any real use to the town.
Hydroelectric power is far more efficient than the old water mills.

stimulus.textEn:
Text 1: A critic praises the new museum's vast empty halls, arguing that the space itself invites quiet reflection and gives each artwork room to breathe.

Text 2: A regular visitor complains that the same echoing halls feel cold and unfinished, as if the builders had simply run out of paintings to hang on the bare walls.

items.prompt: Based on the texts, how would the visitor in Text 2 most likely characterize the empty halls praised in Text 1?

choices.text: As a flaw rather than a virtue.
As far too crowded with art.
As irrelevant to the museum's real purpose.
As clear proof of careful design.

stimulus.textEn: A small town tracked its water use over a single dry summer. In June, before any restrictions, households used an average of 400 liters a day. After the town introduced tiered pricing in July, average use fell to 280 liters, and by August it had dropped further to just 230 liters.

items.prompt: Which choice best describes data presented in the text?

choices.text: Households used the least water in June.
The pricing system had no measurable effect on water use.
Water use rose sharply over the course of the summer.
Average household water use declined steadily after pricing changes were introduced.

stimulus.textEn: The lighthouse keeper's logbook, kept faithfully for thirty years, records the weather every single day but mentions visitors only four times. On each of those four dates, the handwriting grows noticeably larger and the entries run unusually long, crowded with detail about conversations that lasted late into the night.

items.prompt: Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.text: The keeper stopped recording the weather whenever guests arrived.
The lighthouse received visitors nearly every week.
The keeper disliked visitors and tried to avoid mentioning them.
The keeper found visits rare and memorable enough to record at unusual length.

stimulus.textEn: Although she had rehearsed the difficult lecture more times than she could count, the very moment she stepped into the bright lights of the stage she ______ that the sharpest question would come from the youngest student present.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: An economist claims that a city's new bike-share program reduced car traffic downtown. Skeptics point out that traffic also tends to fall whenever fuel prices rise, so she needs evidence that separates the effect of the bikes from that of other possible causes.

items.prompt: Which finding would most directly support the economist's claim?

choices.text: Traffic fell downtown even during months when fuel prices stayed flat.
Downtown parking remained just as expensive as before.
Fuel prices happened to rise sharply the same year the program began.
The bike-share program proved popular in several nearby towns.

stimulus.textEn: The small research team ______ been working in near-total isolation for eighteen months, communicating with the outside world only through a single satellite link that often went dead for days at a stretch.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: While researching a presentation, a student took the following notes: (1) The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system. (2) It stretches over 2,300 kilometres. (3) It is home to more than 1,500 species of fish. (4) Rising sea temperatures have caused several mass bleaching events.

items.prompt: The student wants to emphasize the biological diversity of the reef. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: Home to more than 1,500 species of fish, the Great Barrier Reef supports an extraordinary diversity of life.
The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching over 2,300 kilometres.
Rising sea temperatures have caused several mass bleaching events on the Great Barrier Reef.
The Great Barrier Reef, which stretches over 2,300 kilometres, is the world's largest coral reef system.

stimulus.textEn:
A student researching streetlights took the following notes:
- A town replaced its old orange streetlights with bright white LED lights.
- Energy use for street lighting then dropped by more than half.
- But some residents complained the new light felt harsh and made sleep harder.

items.prompt: The student wants to give a balanced account of the switch to LED streetlights. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: The LED lights cut energy use by more than half, though some residents found the harsh light hard to sleep under.
Energy use for the town's street lighting dropped by more than half.
Some residents complained that the new light simply felt too harsh.
The town replaced its old orange streetlights with bright white LED lights.

stimulus.textEn: The scientist ______ first proposed the idea was at first widely ignored, yet within a single generation her once-radical hypothesis had become the standard explanation taught in nearly every classroom.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: Whenever the power failed at the remote research station, the scientists noticed something strange: within minutes, the readings from their most sensitive instrument grew far steadier than usual. Only later did they realize that the building's own generator had been the source of a faint vibration disturbing it all along.

items.prompt: Which choice is best supported by the text?

choices.text: The scientists had deliberately switched the generator off.
The power failures permanently damaged the instrument.
The generator had unintentionally been interfering with the instrument's readings.
The instrument worked best while the generator was running.

stimulus.textEn:
A student researching a self-taught artist took the following notes:
- She painted almost nothing during the first forty years of her life.
- She began painting only after an illness left her unable to keep farming.
- Within a single decade her work hung in major museums.

items.prompt: The student wants to emphasize how late and unexpected the artist's success was. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

choices.text: She had spent the first forty years of her life without painting much at all.
The artist eventually became fairly well known for her paintings.
An illness once left the artist no longer able to continue farming.
After barely painting for the first forty years of her life, she saw her work reach major museums within a decade.

stimulus.textEn: When the printing press arrived in Europe, many scholars feared that cheap, abundant books would make people lazy and forgetful, no longer bothering to memorize anything. Centuries later, similar fears greeted the pocket calculator, and later still, the search engine. Each new tool, critics warned, would weaken the very minds it was meant to serve.

items.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: New information technologies have repeatedly provoked fears that they would weaken human thinking.
Search engines have finally proven these old fears correct.
Scholars have always opposed the spread of books.
The printing press was more harmful to memory than the calculator.

stimulus.textEn: Honeybees communicate the location of food through a movement that researchers call the waggle dance. The direction of the dance, measured relative to the sun, indicates the direction of the flowers, while the duration of each waggle signals just how far away those flowers lie.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the duration of each waggle indicate?

choices.text: The current position of the sun.

stimulus.textEn: The conference drew researchers from three strikingly different cities ______ Nairobi, Kenya; Lima, Peru; and Hanoi, Vietnam, each delegation bringing field data that existed nowhere else in the world.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: After months of heated debate the planners finally settled on a single unifying theme for the redesigned downtown park ______ water, expressed through fountains, shallow winding streams, and a long reflecting pool running down its center.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: She is one of the few biologists who ______ consistently argued that the fungal networks threading beneath a forest floor deserve every bit as much protection as the towering trees rising above them.

items.prompt: Which choice completes the text so that it conforms to the conventions of Standard English?

stimulus.textEn: The lecture spends nearly a full hour carefully building a case for one elegant theory, then ends with a single quiet question that, if taken seriously, would quietly undo the entire argument just constructed.

items.prompt: Which choice best describes the function of the final question?

choices.text: It neatly summarizes the lecture's main points.
It simply thanks the audience for attending.
It unsettles the very case the lecture has just built.
It supplies further evidence for the theory.

stimulus.textEn: Long after the argument itself was over, he continued to harbor a quiet resentment, never mentioning it aloud but never quite letting it go either.

items.prompt: As used in the text, what does the word 'harbor' most nearly mean?

choices.text: to bring a boat to dock

stimulus.textEn: A language does not die all at once. It fades as the last fluent speakers grow old, as children choose instead the tongues of school and television, and as words for local plants, tools, and weather slip quietly out of use. Each lost word, linguists warn, carries with it a small, unrepeatable way of seeing the world.

items.prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

choices.text: Languages disappear gradually, and each loss erases a unique way of understanding the world.
Television is solely responsible for the loss of languages.
Only words for plants and tools are ever truly lost.
Languages die suddenly the moment their last speaker dies.
